responseSet.title: Psychotherapy Training in Child Psychiatry
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy Training in Child Psychiatry

Tony Jaffa

Tony Jaffa

The Tavistock Clinic
London NW3 513A

Tony Jaffa  

This is an account of a child psychiatrist's first experience of supervised individual psychotherapy with children. It describes a progression from preconceptions and caricaturisation of therapy and therapist, to finding an effective way of working. The progress is through experiencing psychotherapy and supervision sessions, as well as reading relevant material. The psychotherapy was effective both clinically and as an introduction to the area of psychodynamic practice.

responseSet.title: What Nursery School Teachers ask us about Psychoanalytic Consultations in preschools. Edited by Erna Furman. Emotions and Behaviour Monographs — Monograph No. 5.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Nursery School Teachers ask us about Psychoanalytic Consultations in preschools. Edited by Erna Furman. Emotions and Behaviour Monographs — Monograph No. 5.

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell  

As I came to the end of this book I found myself feeling sad. I wished they had tackled more topics. This wish that the book had been longer was something of a surprise as, when I had started reading, it was an effort to keep going. Initially the book was dull, stating the obvious. However, perseverance was rewarded and from the end of the second chapter the book began to come to life.

What was wrong initially? Chapter One concentrates on the role parents and teachers play in the life of the young child, and is a résumé of the differences between mother/child and teacher/child relationships. There are some useful distinctions, but it is written very simply, far beneath the sophistication of most qualified teachers, I would suggest. Then Chapter Two on the father/child relationship was chauvinist and is likely to arouse some hostility, if the reader does not give up because the style is patronising and heavy.

The chapters that follow in Part I gradually speak with a different voice. There is the sense that the authors are now speaking from the experience of consultation and clinical work rather than giving their own concepts. The problems of separation for children and how important it is that it is handled as sensitively as possible is made very clear. The distinction between physical separation and a mastery of the separation experience is useful and explained in a helpful way. The clinical example is used with skill to show how separation problems can prevent a child beginning to learn, and how consultation can facilitate the teachers by enabling them to rethink their approach and make sense of the children's behaviour. Stress and discipline in nursery classes are also discussed in a sympathetic way, with creative useful comments that clearly come out of long experience. The chapter on aggression in preschool children is lively and concise and has a practical application which is of value. The same is true for the account of helping young children with their sexuality and sexual differences.

responseSet.title: Basic Anxieties Affecting Psychiatric Staff and their Attitudes to Psychotic Patients
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Basic Anxieties Affecting Psychiatric Staff and their Attitudes to Psychotic Patients

Irene Sinanoglou

Irene Sinanoglou

37 Creighton Road
London NW6 6EE

Irene Sinanoglou  

Institutions can be used by their individual members to strengthen mechanisms of defence, especially against early paranoid and depressive anxieties. This is not to imply that institutions become psychotic, but that we would expect to find in them manifestations of unreality, splitting, hostility, suspicion and other forms of maladaptive behaviour which are the equivalent of psychotic symptoms of an individual.

This paper describes some of the attitudes of those who care for psychotic patients in a hospital setting. These attitudes seem to be influenced by basic fears that are provoked by the relationship with the patients and by coming in contact with their needs and disturbance from a position of responsibility. Such fears are accompanied by phantasies and are related to specific defence mechanisms.

responseSet.title: Impasse and Interpretation. Therapeutic and anti-therapeutic factors in the psychoanalytic treatment of psychotic, borderline and neurotic patients. By Herbert Rosenfeld Tavistock Pp. vii & 324 £29.95 handback; £13.95 paperback
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Impasse and Interpretation. Therapeutic and anti-therapeutic factors in the psychoanalytic treatment of psychotic, borderline and neurotic patients. By Herbert RosenfeldTavistock Pp. vii & 324 £29.95 handback; £13.95 paperback

Richard Carvalho

Richard Carvalho

Richard Carvalho  

This is a moving book. It is written in a direct style which leaves one with a feeling of having been spoken to. This involvement, the meticulous detail in which the many clinical examples are examined, and the warm compassion extended both to the patient and to the therapist provide the sort of cathartic experience usually available only in an analytic session or supervision. This impressions is enhanced by the touching autobiographical account of Rosenfeld's own development and work in relation to psychotic patients, which forms the first part of the book.

The book is divided into four further parts, the fifth comprising a summarising conclusion. A helpful historical survey of the treatment of psychotic states by psychoanalysis is added in an appendix. This device, together with the use of extensive notes at the end of many chapters, makes possible the inclusion of a lot of review material that would otherwise obscure the book's main purpose. This is the study of some of the factors which lead to theraputic impasse, arising both from pathological processes in the patient and from failures of technique in the therapist which militate against understanding and containment. Central to Rosenfeld's considerations are the axioms, first that there is a sane part in all patients, no matter how psychotic, which is trying to communicate; and second that, in contrast to Searles and Winnicott for instance, no modification of basic psychoanalytic technique is required in their treatment: interpretation suffices.

Specific contributions to therapeutic impasse are examined under sections 2, 3 and 4 of the book. Part two examines some anti-therapeutic factors in the therapist which are exemplified by clinical material in chapter 3. Here the therapist is unable to hear what the patient is communicating because of her difficulties in moving with the shifts in the transference. She has prejudices concerning what the material is about (envy and the weekend rather than her inability to hear what is being said) which are compounded with her difficulties in hearing criticism, so that she is unable to allow the patient to correct her.

responseSet.title: Change in the Group-Analytic Setting
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Change in the Group-Analytic Setting

Dennis Brown

Dennis Brown

The Group-Analytic Practice
88 Montagu Mansions,
London, W1H 1LF.

Dennis Brown  

The paper attempts to illustrate how the group provides the setting for both analysis of the interaction of internal and external objects, and the activation of mutual responsibility and concern. Together these allow the resolution of neurotic blocks to communication and at the same time promote individual maturity. There is interaction between the nature of the setting, relationships, and changes within the Group as perceived by its members. Foulkes in Group Analysis extends and adapts psychoanalytic theory and practice into the group context. As well as facilitating insight and individual restructuring, it offers direct, shared experience of changing the ways individuals relate to each other. People become more aware of themselves and each other, become more mutually responsible and capable of a more mature balance between selfishness and compassion.

responseSet.title: The Psychotherapeutic Treatment of a Psychotic Child with Marked Autistic Features
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychotherapeutic Treatment of a Psychotic Child with Marked Autistic Features

Laura Comelli

Laura Comelli

Via del Pellegrini 18
20122 Milan, Italy.

Laura Comelli  

This is an account of psychotherapy with a psychotic boy for whom the diagnostic prognostications had been very gloomy. For one thing, a previous analysis had broken down due to difficulties with the parents; for another, the child was nine years of age, and his psychosis was so severe and long-standing as to seem intractable. The author found that the main difficulty in working with this child was the autism associated with his psychotic pathology. The core feature of all autism is the disturbance in the primary relationship with the suckling mother. For this difficulty to be explored within the infantile transference of psychoanalytic psychotherapy, an orderly setting had first to be established.

The usual psychoanalytic child guidance methods were somewhat modified in order to cope with the exigencies presented by this case. For example, as the result of the parents' clashes with the colleagues working with them, the child's therapist worked directly with the parents so that they began to take an interest in their child, and also to take responsibility for the regularity and continuance of his attendance for psychotherapy. There were also continuous adaptations of the usual psychoanalytic methods of psychotherapy, the need for which becomes clear as the paper proceeds. It is demonstrated how, by patience, perseverence and willingness to adapt to and learn from them, a therapist can help some seemingly hopeless psychotic children to function relatively normally.

responseSet.documentRef: Britton, R. (1987) Bloomsbury/Freud. The Letters of James and Alix Strachey Walter Kendrick Chatto Windus 1924-1925 Edited by Perry Meisel and Walter Kendrick. Chatto & Windus £14.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:85-88
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Britton, R. (1987) Bloomsbury/Freud. The Letters of James and Alix Strachey Walter Kendrick Chatto Windus 1924-1925 Edited by Perry Meisel and Walter Kendrick. Chatto & Windus £14.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:85-88
responseSet.documentRefXML: Britton, R. (1987) Bloomsbury/Freud. The Letters of James and Alix Strachey Walter Kendrick Chatto Windus 1924-1925 Edited by Perry Meisel and Walter Kendrick. Chatto & Windus £14.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:85-88
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1987 3 1 85-88 Bloomsbury/Freud. The Letters of James and Alix Strachey Walter Kendrick Chatto Windus 1924-1925 Edited by Perry Meisel and Walter Kendrick. Chatto & Windus £14. Ronald Britton
responseSet.title: Bloomsbury/Freud. The Letters of James and Alix Strachey Walter Kendrick Chatto Windus 1924-1925 Edited by Perry Meisel and Walter Kendrick. Chatto & Windus £14.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bloomsbury/Freud. The Letters of James and Alix Strachey Walter Kendrick Chatto Windus1924-1925 Edited by Perry Meisel and Walter Kendrick. Chatto & Windus £14.

Ronald Britton

Ronald Britton

Ronald Britton  

“He was brilliant but there was too much Chopin”. This verdict on a performance by a pianist Georg Bartram in Berlin on 8 May 1925 was made characteristically by Alix Strachey and communicated to her husband James by letter at home in London. The letter containing this is one of a selection made by the editors from the correspondence which passed between the Stracheys during the period September 1924 to September 1925, a year of separation occasioned by Alix Strachey's analysis with Karl Abraham in Berlin. The analysis was terminated sadly by Abraham's illness and unexpected death in 1925. This was her second analysis; her first had been with Freud concurrently with her husband in Vienna and had qualified her as an analyst. Neurosis continued to plague her and she did not have enough emotional ‘Spielraum’ (elbow room), in her own words, to be an analyst. At Freud's suggestion she went to Abraham for more help.

The judgement on the pianist was similar to the one she made on a more famous performer, Edwin Fischer. “His technique was terrific” but “as soon as one notices temperament all is lost. Then he banged away at Chopin, I condemned the whole crew-piano, & composer.” I quote this because it conveys Alix's lively engaging style, and her seeking after a refined sensibility. This search at its best makes her discriminating but at its worst shades off into a fastidiousness and emotional anorexia, with distaste for direct emotional expression. Perhaps this foreshadows the idiosyncratic asceticism which she shared with James later in life, according to Michael Holroyd, Lytton Strachey's biographer. This quality may give some substance to Bettelheim's criticism of the Stracheys’ great translation of Freud which forms the Standard Edition. Bettelheim's criticisms are strongly rebutted by the editors of this book in the Epilogue, where they describe it unequivocally as “the greatest achievement of its kind in the twentieth century”. One of many fascinating glimpses provided by this correspondence is into the beginnings of that enterprise in the form of its forerunner, The Collected Papers of Freud, which were in translation during this time. The letters are punctuated by James's descriptions of his meetings with Joan Riviere and Ernest Jones, his collaborators; and proofs cross and recross the North Sea between the Stracheys. At one point the manuscript of The Wolf Man’ appears lost in the post for a time.

responseSet.documentRef: Birksted-Breen, D. (1987) Handbook of Psychotherapy for Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia. Edited by Garner D. Garfïnkel E. Guilfordand Press. Pp. 592. £35.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:93-95
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Birksted-Breen, D. (1987) Handbook of Psychotherapy for Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia. Edited by Garner D. Garfïnkel E. Guilfordand Press. Pp. 592. £35.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:93-95
responseSet.documentRefXML: Birksted-Breen, D. (1987) Handbook of Psychotherapy for Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia. Edited by Garner D. Garfïnkel E. Guilfordand Press. Pp. 592. £35.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:93-95
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1987 3 1 93-95 Handbook of Psychotherapy for Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia. Edited by Garner D. Garfïnkel E. Guilfordand Press. Pp. 592. £35. Dana Birksted-Breen
responseSet.title: Handbook of Psychotherapy for Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia. Edited by Garner D. Garfïnkel E. Guilfordand Press. Pp. 592. £35.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Handbook of Psychotherapy for Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia. Edited by Garner D.Garfïnkel E.GuilfordandPress. Pp. 592. £35.

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen  

As the title suggests, this book is a collection of papers covering a range of psychotherapies used in the treatment of anorexia nervosa and bulimia. The book deals almost exclusively with these disturbances in women. The term psychotherapy is here used to include not only psychodynamically oriented psychotherapy but such radically different approaches as cognitive and behavioural methods and even self-help groups. Group and family therapy also each form a section. To complete the picture some authors advocate a “multicomponent treatment program because of the need for change at intrapsychic, family and peer group levels” (Strober & Yager, p. 367).

So here we have a book about disorders which a number of authors stress are multidetermined and heterogeneous in nature; and we have a multiplicity of treatment approaches, the outcome of which many of the authors are cautious about. What is one really comparing? Can one compare the efficacy of one method versus another when premises and ideologies are so different? Weight gain is usually the criterion used. It is a simple and objective criterion and that makes life easy. But can the “result” of the said “psychotherapy” really be measured purely in terms of weight gain? Is that not using the same currency as the anorexic or bulimic person is using?

Of course a dangerously emaciated person needs to be helped out of the immediate impasse she is in, but can one really consider that putting on weight over a period of a few weeks is in itself anything more than a cure of the immediate physical condition? It is “first aid”, not psychotherapy. It is not because the anorexic woman says she wants little that she should be offered little. On the contrary I believe that because the anorexic woman can only allow herself little she needs to be offered plenty — plenty of time and plenty of space (which is an altogether different matter to force-feeding).

93

responseSet.title: Suicide in Adolescence
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Suicide in Adolescence

Eglé Laufer

Eglé Laufer

48 Abbey Gardens
London Nw8 9AT

Eglé Laufer  

Suicide is a violent action directed at the person's own body. This action is motivated by a fantasy and as such is defined as a break with reality which constitutes a psychotic episode. Adolescence is considered next, and is seen as a period during which radical changes take place in the person's relationship to his or her body. The fact that suicide attempts in young people almost always first occur after puberty has been reached is then used to show the meaning of the suicidal act as a violent attack on the adolescent's new sexual body. Insofar as this holds for every suicide attempt, however minor, the argument is put forward that a suicide attempt is always a sign of serious disruption and deadlock in the adolescent's development to normal adulthood. In order to help the adolescent with the developmental deadlock the need for regular, and in particular long-term, psychoanalytic psychotherapy is stressed as a matter of urgency in order to avoid later pathological development from taking place.

responseSet.title: Some Problems in the Clinical Application of Attachment Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Problems in the Clinical Application of Attachment Theory

Victoria Hamilton

Victoria Hamilton

London NW3 5BA

Victoria Hamilton  

This paper addresses some of the problems which arise when attempts are made to apply research findings to clinical work. The problem of the relevance of research to psychoanalysis is a general one and arises in relation to findings from related areas such as infancy, child development, bereavement etc. In this paper, the author tries to pinpoint some specific areas of difficulty in integrating concepts from attachment theory as developed by Dr fohn Bowlby into the clinical situation. Three areas of conflict are delineated relating to essential differences in skill (including personality), task and setting. Particular emphasis is placed upon the dynamics of the transference relationship and its destabilising effects on a patient's sense of security as this has become lodged in his defensive system. These thoughts were stimulated by a workshop conducted by the author at the International Attachment Conference held in Regents College on 26-27 June 1987 to celebrate Dr Bowlby's eightieth birthday.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Theories on Drug Dependence: A Critical Review
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Theories on Drug Dependence: A Critical Review

Kamran Ghaffari

Kamran Ghaffari

Isleworth Middlesex TW7 6AF

Kamran Ghaffari  

The contribution of psychoanalysis to the understanding of drug dependence is critically reviewed and examined from historical and theoretical perspectives. In the earlier theories the emphasis was primarily on the regressive and libidinal aspects of the effects of drugs. The level of conceptualization was that of the instinct theory, the motivation being that of pleasure. In recent years there has been a shift towards viewing the motivation as attempts at self-medication and self-care rather than primarily pleasure-seeking. The lack of empirical evidence in support of these theories is noted.
